meta.name: Posterior STEMI — ST Depression V1–V3 with Tall R Waves; Posterior Leads Confirm

patient.name: Vincent Romano
patient.chiefComplaint: Substernal chest pressure with radiation to the back and a feeling of indigestion that started about 50 minutes ago after dinner; not relieved by antacids.

history.hpi: 60-year-old male was finishing dinner with his wife when he developed a deep pressure-like discomfort in the mid-chest with radiation through to the back between his shoulder blades. He initially attributed this to indigestion from a heavy meal and took two doses of liquid antacid with no relief. Over 30 minutes the discomfort intensified, he became diaphoretic and nauseated, and he asked his wife to call 911. Patient describes the pain as 7/10, deep, pressure-like, with associated nausea and light-headedness. Denies vomiting, denies positional or pleuritic component, denies sharp tearing pain (no aortic dissection features). He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia and was a smoker until 5 years ago.
history.lastOralIntake: Large pasta dinner with red wine ~1 hour ago
history.events: Onset of substernal pressure with back radiation while finishing dinner ~50 minutes ago; progressed to diaphoresis, nausea, and lightheadedness despite antacids. Wife called 911.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 60-year-old male, chest pain and indigestion, conscious and breathing. Wife on scene at single-family home.
scene.location: Single-family home, dining room. Patient seated at the table, leaning back in the chair. Adequate lighting. Clear path to the front door.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Pets contained. Wife is calm and helpful. Adequate space.
scene.bystanders: Wife present, gives history and brings the medication bottles. Confirms no PDE5 inhibitor use in the household.
visualCues: Patient seated at dining table, leaning back, hand on mid-chest
Pale and diaphoretic; collar of shirt visibly damp
Half-eaten pasta plate and partially full wine glass on table
Empty bottle of liquid antacid on the table beside him
Patient grimacing intermittently and rubbing the area between his shoulder blades

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives to find a 60-year-old male seated at his dining table, pale, diaphoretic, and clutching his mid-chest. He insists 'this is just bad indigestion' but his appearance tells a different story. Pain has been present for ~50 minutes. The teaching point sets up here: this is going to look like an ACS evaluation, but the standard 12-lead is going to require careful interpretation because the infarct is on the posterior wall — ST depression V1–V3 with tall R waves, not the usual elevation pattern.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Strong, regular radial pulse; cool, pale, diaphoretic; cap refill 2 sec
otherFindings: Patient leaning back, hand on mid-chest, intermittently rubbing mid-back
No tearing or migrating pain features (rules against dissection)
Symptoms worsened despite antacids — argues against simple indigestion
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I think it's just indigestion. The wine maybe. But it won't go away. I have a really uncomfortable pressure right here, and it goes through to my back.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; do NOT accept 'just indigestion' framing — treat as possible ACS
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Goal #1 + Inclusion Criteria — chest pain or discomfort of suspected cardiac origin, atypical/vague complaints common.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Identify ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) quickly. Inclusion Criteria: Chest pain or discomfort in other areas of the body (e.g., arm, jaw, epigastrium) of suspected cardiac origin, shortness of breath, associated or unexplained sweating, nausea, vomiting, or dizziness.

expectedActions.shortAction: OPQRST + SAMPLE; document onset, screen for PDE5 use
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Goal #2 + step 7 — onset time and PDE5 screen prior to nitrates.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Care Goals: Determine the time of symptom onset. The use of nitrates should be avoided in any patient who has used a phosphodiesterase inhibitor within the past 48 hours.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor; SpO2; defib pads on (high-risk)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 2 + Patient Safety #3 — monitor and defib pads on high-risk patients.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Assess the patient's cardiac rhythm and immediately address pulseless rhythms, symptomatic tachycardia, or symptomatic bradycardia. Patient Safety Considerations: Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients.

expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 — 12-lead within 10 minutes is the diagnostic tool.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The 12-lead EKG is the primary diagnostic tool that identifies a STEMI; it is imperative that EMS clinicians routinely acquire a 12-lead EKG within 10 minutes for all patients exhibiting signs and symptoms of ACS.

expectedActions.shortAction: Aspirin 324 mg PO chewed (4 × 81 mg chewable, non-enteric)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 5 — chewable, non-enteric ASA 162–325 mg.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer aspirin; chewable, non-enteric-coated aspirin preferred (162–325 mg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish IV access (18 ga AC preferred)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 6 — IV access required prior to nitrate or analgesic.

transitions.if: 12-lead acquired showing ST depression V1–V3 + tall R
transitions.condition: Standard 12-lead EKG acquired; shows ST depression in V1, V2, V3 with abnormally tall R waves in V1–V2 and upright T waves — classic posterior wall pattern. No ST elevation on the standard 12-lead. Crew must now decide whether to obtain posterior leads.

phases.name: ST depression V1–V3 + tall R waves
phases.description: Standard 12-lead shows ST depression of 2–3 mm in V1, V2, V3 with R waves > S waves in V1 and V2 and upright T waves in those leads. There is NO ST elevation on the standard 12-lead. The reciprocal pattern (anterior depression + tall R wave) IS the posterior STEMI footprint. The decision now is whether to acquire posterior leads (V7, V8, V9) — if any show ≥ 0.5 mm ST elevation, posterior STEMI is confirmed. Crew should also consider this could represent acute posterior MI from circumflex or RCA occlusion.
phases.triggerCondition: Standard 12-lead acquired showing ST depression V1–V3 with tall R waves and upright T waves — pattern suspicious for posterior STEMI; posterior leads needed for confirmation.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Regular pulse; pale, cool, diaphoretic; cap refill 2 sec
otherFindings: Standard 12-lead: ST depression 2–3 mm V1–V3; R/S > 1 in V1; upright T in V1–V2
Pain unchanged at 7/10; patient now more anxious
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: It hasn't gone away. It almost feels worse now.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize ST depression V1–V3 + tall R as possible posterior STEMI
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #2 + step 4 — perform serial EKGs (especially with clinical changes); the field clinician must recognize STEMI in non-elevation patterns to drive activation.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted). The 12-lead EKG is the primary diagnostic tool that identifies a STEMI; it is imperative that EMS clinicians routinely acquire a 12-lead EKG within 10 minutes for all patients exhibiting signs and symptoms of ACS. The EKG may be transmitted for remote interpretation by a physician or screened for STEMI by properly trained EMS clinicians or other healthcare providers with or without the assistance of computer-interpretation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Acquire posterior leads V7, V8, V9 — confirm with ≥ 0.5 mm ST elevation
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 + Patient Safety #2 — serial EKGs encouraged for non-diagnostic standard 12-leads; posterior leads confirm posterior wall MI.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance of serial EKGs is encouraged for symptomatic patients with EKGs initially non-diagnostic for STEMI. All EKGs should be made available to treating personnel at the receiving hospital, whether hand delivered as hard copy or transmitted from the field.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transmit 12-lead for remote physician interpretation if posterior pattern uncertain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 — EKG may be transmitted for remote interpretation; all EKGs to receiving hospital.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The EKG may be transmitted for remote interpretation by a physician or screened for STEMI by properly trained EMS clinicians or other healthcare providers with or without the assistance of computer-interpretation. All EKGs should be made available to treating personnel at the receiving hospital, whether hand delivered as hard copy or transmitted from the field.

transitions.if: Posterior leads confirm STEMI; activate alert
transitions.condition: V7-V9 posterior leads acquired showing ≥ 0.5 mm ST elevation; posterior STEMI confirmed; STEMI alert called.

transitions.if: Posterior leads not acquired; treated as NSTEMI/ischemia
transitions.condition: Crew interprets standard 12-lead as 'NSTEMI / ischemia / non-diagnostic' and does not acquire posterior leads. STEMI alert is not called. Patient is transported as a non-STEMI cardiac patient — possibly to a non-PCI hospital — and reperfusion is delayed.

phases.name: Posterior STEMI confirmed; alert called
phases.description: Posterior leads V7, V8, V9 demonstrate ≥ 1 mm ST elevation, confirming acute posterior wall STEMI consistent with circumflex or RCA territory occlusion. Crew calls STEMI alert with the receiving PCI center, transmits both standard and posterior 12-leads, gives nitroglycerin after PDE5 screen and acceptable BP, and prepares rapid transport.
phases.triggerCondition: Posterior leads V7–V9 confirm posterior STEMI; STEMI activation indicated; ASA given, IV in place, PDE5 ruled out.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Regular pulse; cool, diaphoretic; cap refill 2 sec
otherFindings: Posterior leads V7-V9: ST elevation 1 mm — confirms posterior STEMI
Standard + posterior 12-leads transmitted to PCI center
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Yeah, it's still really uncomfortable. Are we going to the hospital now?

expectedActions.shortAction: Activate STEMI alert; transmit/notify PCI center; flag 'posterior'
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Goal #3 + step 4 — STEMI activation drives door-to-balloon; advance notification.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Activate hospital-based STEMI system of care. Advance notification should be provided to the receiving hospital for patients identified as having a STEMI. All EKGs should be made available to treating personnel at the receiving hospital, whether hand delivered as hard copy or transmitted from the field.

expectedActions.shortAction: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL (SBP 138, no PDE5 use); may repeat q3–5 min if SBP > 100
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7 — NTG only after PDE5 screen and SBP > 100.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (SL), can repeat q (quaque, every) 3–5 minutes if SBP greater than 100 mmHg. The use of nitrates should be avoided in any patient who has used a phosphodiesterase inhibitor within the past 48 hours.

expectedActions.shortAction: Minimize scene time; rapid transport to PCI center
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Educational Pearl — ischemic burden time drives morbidity/mortality.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Considerations: Acute coronary syndrome may present with atypical pain, vague or only generalized complaints. Ischemic burden time is a risk for morbidity and mortality, EMS can help decrease first medical contact to intervention time/reflow by efficient scripting/training of safely minimizing scene time.

expectedActions.shortAction: Defib pads on; watch for VT/VF, AV blocks (RCA/Cx territory), serial 12-leads
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #1, #2, #3, #4 — observe for dysrhythmias/decompensation, serial EKGs, defib pads, configure VT/VF auto-alert.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension. Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted). Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients. Consider configuring monitor/defibrillator to allow automatic VT/VF alert.

transitions.if: STEMI alerted, en route to PCI
transitions.condition: STEMI activation called, transport initiated to PCI-capable center, NTG given with appropriate hemodynamic monitoring.

phases.name: Transport to PCI center
phases.description: Patient en route to PCI-capable receiving facility with STEMI alert active. Pain reduced after second NTG. Hemodynamically stable. Crew provides bedside handoff with full clinical picture: posterior STEMI on V7–V9, time of symptom onset, treatment timeline.
phases.triggerCondition: STEMI alert called and transport to PCI-capable hospital initiated; ongoing ACS care en route.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Regular pulse; warmer skin; less diaphoretic
otherFindings: Pain reduced from 7/10 to 4/10 after 2nd NTG
Repeat 12-lead unchanged — persistent posterior STEMI pattern
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: It's a little better. I really thought it was just dinner.

expectedActions.shortAction: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL q3–5 min while SBP > 100; reassess
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7 — NTG may be repeated q3–5 min while SBP > 100.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (SL), can repeat q (quaque, every) 3–5 minutes if SBP greater than 100 mmHg.

expectedActions.shortAction: Early notify hospital of any change; deliver standard + posterior EKGs
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 11 + Documentation — early notification and EKG delivery are protocol requirements.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs. Key Documentation Elements: The time of symptom onset; The time of patient contact by EMS to the time of 12-lead EKG acquisition; The time aspirin (ASA) administered, or reason why not given; The time of STEMI notification.

expectedActions.shortAction: If pain unresponsive to NTG, opiate analgesic per Pain Management
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 9 — opiates as alternative when NTG inadequate.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If the pain is unresponsive to nitrates, opiates are an acceptable alternative. Morphine should be used with caution in unstable angina (UA)/non-STEMI due to an association with increased mortality.

phases.name: Posterior leads not done; STEMI missed
phases.description: Crew interprets the standard 12-lead as 'NSTEMI / ischemia / non-diagnostic' and does not acquire posterior leads. STEMI alert is not called. Patient is transported as a non-STEMI cardiac patient — possibly bypassing a closer PCI-capable center for the closest community ED. Door-to-balloon clock effectively does not start until the receiving ED ultimately recognizes the pattern themselves and rooms transfer the patient. This is the canonical missed posterior STEMI debrief.
phases.triggerCondition: Posterior leads were not acquired; ST depression V1–V3 with tall R waves was misinterpreted as NSTEMI / ischemia / non-diagnostic; STEMI activation never occurred.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Regular pulse; pale, cool, persistently diaphoretic
otherFindings: Receiving ED will need to re-acquire 12-lead and posterior leads
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: It still really hurts. Are they going to be able to do something at the hospital?

expectedActions.shortAction: Acquire posterior leads V7–V9 now; reroute to PCI; activate STEMI alert
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 + Patient Safety #2 — serial EKGs encouraged; posterior leads needed for non-elevation pattern; STEMI activation must follow.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance of serial EKGs is encouraged for symptomatic patients with EKGs initially non-diagnostic for STEMI. Patient Safety Considerations: Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted). Activate hospital-based STEMI system of care.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transmit standard + posterior 12-leads for remote interpretation
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 — EKG may be transmitted for remote interpretation; all EKGs to receiving hospital.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The EKG may be transmitted for remote interpretation by a physician or screened for STEMI by properly trained EMS clinicians or other healthcare providers with or without the assistance of computer-interpretation. All EKGs should be made available to treating personnel at the receiving hospital, whether hand delivered as hard copy or transmitted from the field.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document time pattern recognized; reason posterior leads delayed
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Documentation — contact-to-EKG, ASA timing, STEMI notification time required.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Documentation Elements: The time of patient contact by EMS to the time of 12-lead EKG acquisition; The time aspirin (ASA) administered, or reason why not given; The time of STEMI notification.
